topic: When a government inspection reveals what a manual HR system has been missing
format: Walk through a day-in-the-life of an HR team managing this manually — show every broken step in sequence
post:
The Ministry of Labour inspector arrives at 9am unannounced.

Your HR manager is already sweating — not because anything is wrong, but because proving it's right will take the rest of the day.

She pulls out three binders. Starts cross-referencing handwritten leave records with a spreadsheet that hasn't been updated since March. The inspector waits. The clock runs.

This is the hidden cost nobody talks about.

Not the fine. Not the legal fees. The four hours your most capable person spent reconstructing records that should have been one click away.

Most compliance failures aren't dramatic. They're death by paperwork — the slow grind of systems that were never built to be audited.

If your HR stack can't answer a regulator's question in under five minutes, it's not a documentation problem. It's an infrastructure problem dressed up as an admin problem.

The Ministry of Labour doesn't care how hard your team worked to build those binders. They care what's in them.

Build for the audit before the auditor shows up.
